caveats: a: The principal occupation source describes mine operators, not 213114 contractors.
a: Occupation shares do not directly measure wage-weighted, not-yet-automated tasks.
rho: Technical exploration contractors may adopt faster than equipment-intensive mine-development contractors.
e: The frozen count uses an oilfield-services margin bridge that may misclassify metal-mining contractors.
s5: No observed six-digit transfer-rate series was located.
q: Scarce specialty drilling and geotechnical work may retain more than routine earthmoving or maintenance.

d5: The industry spans commodities and project stages with divergent cycles.
o: Commodity-driven volume effects belong in d5 and are not repeated here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat project, footage-, unit-, day-rate, and time-and-materials services sold to external mine developers and operators, limited to transferable firms plausibly producing $1-10 million of normalized EBITDA.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive mine-owner crews; mineral extraction for the firm's own account; commodity traders; equipment-only dealers; pure laboratories or one-off consultants without recurring field delivery; shells; and firms dominated by coal, oil, gas, or nonmetallic-mineral support.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. contractors providing repeat exploration drilling, mine development, blasting, excavation, dewatering, equipment operation and maintenance, sampling, surveying, geological field support, reclamation, and related services to metal-mine owners.

a.bridge: BLS metal-ore-mining employment, used as the closest commodity-specific task proxy, shows 44.75% construction/extraction workers, 19.39% installation/maintenance/repair workers, 5.76% architecture/engineering, and 3.87% life/physical/social science; broader mining support shows 34.37% extraction workers. I translate the physical majority plus a meaningful technical layer into a minority wage-weighted AI-exposed share concentrated in geological interpretation, proposals, scheduling, reporting, maintenance planning, and compliance. Mine-owner data may overstate engineering exposure for field contractors but understate office work at service firms.
caveats: The principal occupation source describes mine operators, not 213114 contractors.
Occupation shares do not directly measure wage-weighted, not-yet-automated tasks.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit contractor occupation/payroll data and time studies separating field, engineering/geology, safety, dispatch, commercial, and administrative tasks.
a.rationale: AI can materially assist technical and information workflows, while drilling, blasting, excavation, maintenance, sampling, equipment operation, and accountable field safety remain embodied.

d5.bridge: USGS reports the value of U.S. nonfuel mineral production increased 5.6% to $112 billion in 2025, while 2025 U.S. copper mine output fell 5% to 1.0 million metric tons and came from 26 mines; DOE committed $134 million in 2026 to rare-earth recovery projects. I translate mixed current production, strategic-mineral investment, exploration, and reclamation needs into a broad five-year real service-demand range centered on modest growth. Value growth may overstate real activity because prices drove it, while copper alone may understate demand across gold, lithium, rare earths, and other metals.
caveats: Production value is price-sensitive and is not constant-price service quantity.
The industry spans commodities and project stages with divergent cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Mine-by-mine five-year exploration, feasibility, construction, production, closure, and reclamation pipelines mapped to contractor revenue by commodity and basin.
d5.rationale: Electrification, grid investment, defense supply security, precious metals, and domestic critical-mineral policy support activity, but permitting delays, commodity cycles, import competition, project cancellations, and depletion create high variance.

caveats: The frozen count uses an oilfield-services margin bridge that may misclassify metal-mining contractors.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level ownership, revenue mix, customer contracts, normalized EBITDA, operating status, and service-delivery verification for all 14 frozen candidates.
e.rationale: Many independent contractors fit repeat external service, but the frozen population may include captive affiliates, diversified mining-support firms, laboratories, equipment lessors, speculative exploration entities, and firms outside the EBITDA band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 213114 ($591,447,144) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 213114 ($1,713,343,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0776 (margins.json[213]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Oilfield Svcs/Equip. EBITDA/Sales 7.76%); revenue/employee $327,787 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 213114); class <5: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $576,952 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $44,772 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,982,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $153,803 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,176,607 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $324,105 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,470,043 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $890,075 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $70,780,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,492,580 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $119,113,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,243,169 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 14

caveats: Commodity-driven volume effects belong in d5 and are not repeated here.
o.change_evidence: Mine sourcing plans and operating data quantifying insourcing, autonomous drilling/haulage, remote operations, and eliminated contractor work packages.
o.rationale: Remaining work requires accountable operators, field crews, equipment, permits, sampling integrity, and safety systems. Autonomous equipment and mine-owner insourcing can reduce outsourced labor, but software alone cannot deliver most physical field services.

caveats: Scarce specialty drilling and geotechnical work may retain more than routine earthmoving or maintenance.
q.change_evidence: Contract terms, bid histories, renewal pricing, customer concentration, gain-sharing clauses, and post-improvement gross-margin bridges at candidate firms.
q.rationale: Footage, day-rate, and project contracts can retain productivity during their term; technical reputation, safety performance, scarce crews, and specialized equipment support differentiation. Large mine customers and competitive rebids capture savings over time.

caveats: Technical exploration contractors may adopt faster than equipment-intensive mine-development contractors.
rho.change_evidence: Contractor deployments measuring autonomous workflow completion, geologic-model error, report-cycle time, review burden, and labor hours avoided.
rho.rationale: Geological data review, bid preparation, drill-log processing, reporting, planning, and maintenance analytics are plausible five-year applications, but sparse proprietary data, remote connectivity, safety validation, client approvals, and change capacity constrain implementation.

caveats: No observed six-digit transfer-rate series was located.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transactions, owner-age and succession data, and sale-versus-liquidation outcomes for independent U.S. metal-mining contractors.
s5.rationale: Succession, strategic capability acquisition, and mining-cycle consolidation can create sales, while project dependence, environmental liabilities, equipment condition, bonding, and commodity downturns can lead to closure or asset sales rather than qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Strategic and electrification-related mineral demand supports continuing exploration, development, and specialized field-service needs.
principal_weakness: A small eligible universe faces severe commodity-cycle and single-project volatility despite favorable long-term mineral themes.
